sentiment.label: risk_off
sentiment.reason_it: Il sentiment di mercato è risk-off a causa della guerra tra Israele e Iran, con il VIX a 26.06 e il DXY a 100.35. La produzione industriale cinese è aumentata, ma le preoccupazioni sull'inflazione globale sono elevate. Il mercato azionario è in calo, con l'S&P 500 a 6632.19 e lo STOXX 600 a 58.91. Le banche centrali saranno sotto la lente d'ingrandimento questa settimana con le loro decisioni sui tassi.
sentiment.reason_en: Market sentiment is risk-off due to the Israel-Iran war, with VIX at 26.06 and DXY at 100.35. China's industrial production increased, but global inflation concerns are high. Equity markets are down, with S&P 500 at 6632.19 and STOXX 600 at 58.91. Central banks will be in focus this week with rate decisions.

market_impact_summary.it: La guerra tra Israele e Iran ha aumentato le preoccupazioni sull'inflazione globale e ha portato a un aumento del VIX. Il DXY è stabile, mentre l'S&P 500 e lo STOXX 600 sono in calo. Il prezzo del petrolio è sceso a $101.43. Le banche centrali saranno sotto la lente d'ingrandimento questa settimana. BlackRock Investment Institute e Goldman Sachs hanno pubblicato report settimanali che analizzano l'impatto della guerra sui mercati.
market_impact_summary.en: The Israel-Iran war has increased global inflation concerns and led to a rise in VIX. DXY is stable, while S&P 500 and STOXX 600 are down. Oil price is at $101.43. Central banks will be in focus this week. BlackRock Investment Institute and Goldman Sachs have released weekly reports analyzing the war's impact on markets.

audio_script_it:
Benvenuti al consueto briefing mattutino dei mercati. 
Oggi è lunedì 16 marzo 2026, e il sentiment di mercato è decisamente risk-off, con una score di 7 su 10. 
La guerra tra Israele e Iran continua a tenere banco, con l'indice Vix che sale a 26,06 e l'indice del dollaro (DXY) che si attesta a 100,35. 
La produzione industriale cinese ha mostrato un aumento, ma le preoccupazioni sull'inflazione globale sono elevate. 
Il mercato azionario è in calo, con lo Standard and Poor's 500 che ha ceduto lo 0,61% portandosi a quota 6632 punti e lo STOXX 600 che ha perso lo 0,37% arrivando a 58,91 punti.

Passiamo ora ad analizzare i mercati asset per asset. 
Il cambio euro dollaro è sceso dello 0,64% a 1,14. 
L'indice del dollaro (DXY) ha perso lo 0,01% arrivando a 100,35. 
Lo Standard and Poor's 500 ha ceduto lo 0,61% portandosi a quota 6632 punti. 
L'indice Vix è sceso del 4,16% a 26,06. 
L'ETF obbligazionario Treasury (TLT) ha perso lo 0,49% arrivando a 86,54. 
Il rendimento del decennale USA è salito dello 0,28% a 4,28%. 
L'oro è sceso dell'1,22% a 4990,70 dollari l'oncia. 
Bitcoin si attesta intorno ai settantadue mila dollari, con un aumento dello 0,20%. 
Il greggio Brent è sceso dell'1,66% arrivando a 101,43 dollari al barile. 
Lo STOXX 600 ha perso lo 0,37% arrivando a 58,91 punti. 
L'indice Nikkei ha ceduto lo 0,13% arrivando a 53.751 punti. 
L'indice Shanghai ha perso lo 0,26% arrivando a 4084,79 punti.

Sul fronte geopolitico, la guerra tra Israele e Iran continua a preoccupare i mercati. 
Gli eventi di questi giorni hanno portato a un aumento delle preoccupazioni sull'inflazione globale, e le banche centrali saranno sotto la lente d'ingrandimento questa settimana con le loro decisioni sui tassi. 
Il team di ricerca di BlackRock ha pubblicato un report settimanale sull'impatto della guerra sul mercato del petrolio, sottolineando come la situazione possa influire sui prezzi del greggio.

Passiamo ora alle notizie macroeconomiche. 
La Federal Reserve, la Banca Centrale Europea e la Banca d'Inghilterra saranno protagoniste questa settimana con le loro decisioni sui tassi, che potrebbero avere un impatto significativo sui mercati. 
La crescita economica cinese è ripresa, ma le preoccupazioni sull'inflazione globale sono elevate. 
Il governo cinese ha avvertito dei rischi geopolitici, nonostante gli indicatori chiave mostrino una crescita.

Il primo ministro del Regno Unito, Starmer, ha annunciato un pacchetto di sostegno energetico da 50 milioni di sterline per alleviare l'impatto della guerra sul costo della vita. 
La situazione in Medio Oriente continua a essere tesa, con la guerra che entra nella sua terza settimana. 
Gli esperti del Crisis Group offrono una visione a 360 gradi degli effetti del conflitto sulla pace e sulla stabilità globale.

Questa settimana, il presidente degli Stati Uniti incontrerà il primo ministro giapponese, Sanae Takaichi, per discutere della situazione in Medio Oriente e delle tensioni con la Cina. 
La visita del premier giapponese a Washington sarà importante per capire come gli Stati Uniti intendono gestire la crisi.

Sul fronte macroeconomico, il dato sul PCE Core YoY è atteso per il 28 marzo, mentre il GDP QoQ USA è previsto per il 29 aprile. 
La Fed manterrà i tassi invariati al 3,64% in attesa di ulteriori dati macroeconomici.

In conclusione, la settimana sarà caratterizzata da importanti decisioni delle banche centrali e da una situazione geopolitica sempre più complessa. 
I mercati saranno molto volatili e sarà importante monitorare i dati macroeconomici e le notizie geopolitiche.

Per quanto riguarda i dati specifici da monitorare, segnaliamo:
- La decisione sui tassi della Federal Reserve il 22 marzo
- La decisione sui tassi della Banca Centrale Europea il 24 marzo
- Il dato sul PCE Core YoY il 28 marzo
- Il GDP QoQ USA il 29 aprile

Rimanete sintonizzati per ulteriori aggiornamenti.
audio_script_en:
Welcome to your daily morning market briefing.

Market sentiment is risk-off with a score of 7 out of 10, primarily due to the escalating conflict between Israel and Iran. This heightened geopolitical tension has led to a surge in safe-haven assets, with the Vix index currently at 26.06 and the dollar index at 100.35. Despite China's industrial production showing an increase, global inflation concerns remain high, contributing to the cautious market atmosphere. Equity markets are experiencing a downturn, with the Standard and Poor's 500 at 6632.19 and the Stoxx six hundred at 58.91.

Let's take a closer look at the markets, asset by asset. The euro-dollar exchange rate is currently at 1.14, down 0.64% from the previous day. The dollar index, which measures the value of the US dollar against a basket of major currencies, is at 100.35, down 0.01%. The Standard and Poor's 500, a benchmark for US equities, is at 6632.19, down 0.61%. The Vix index, often referred to as the fear index, is at 26.06, down 4.16% but still elevated, indicating ongoing market volatility. US Treasury bonds are seeing a slight decline, with the 20-year Treasury bond ETF at 86.54, down 0.49%, and the 10-year yield at 4.28%, up 0.28%. Gold, a traditional safe-haven asset, is at $4990.70 per ounce, down 1.22%. Bitcoin, on the other hand, is showing resilience, up 0.20% at $72,932. The Brent crude oil price is at $101.43, down 1.66%, reflecting concerns over global demand amidst the Iran-Israel conflict. The Stoxx six hundred, a European equity benchmark, is at 58.91, down 0.37%. In Asia, the Nikkei is at 53,751, down 0.13%, and the Shanghai index is at 4084.79, down 0.26%.

Geopolitics remains at the forefront, with the Israel-Iran conflict significantly impacting market sentiment. The ongoing war has reawakened global inflation fears, particularly due to its potential impact on oil supplies. Any escalation could lead to further disruptions in the oil market, pushing prices higher and exacerbating inflationary pressures. The situation is being closely monitored by investors and policymakers alike, as it poses significant risks to global economic stability.

On the macroeconomic front, central banks are set to take center stage this week with several rate decisions. The Federal Reserve, the European Central Bank, and other major central banks are expected to provide guidance on their monetary policy stances in light of the current geopolitical tensions and inflation concerns. The last available US inflation data, from February, showed a year-over-year CPI of 2.7%, slightly down from 2.8% previously. Core CPI was also at 2.7%, down from 2.9%. However, these figures are considered stale, given that they are over 14 days old. More recent data, such as the PCE Core, which is due on March 28, will be crucial in determining the Fed's next move. The current Fed Funds Rate is 3.64%, unchanged from the previous month.

Global M2 liquidity, a proxy for global money supply, continues to expand, currently at $22.4 trillion, with a year-over-year increase of 4.3%. This expansion in liquidity supports asset prices but also contributes to inflationary pressures.

Looking ahead, several key data points and events are worth monitoring. The US GDP QoQ, due on April 29, will provide insights into the health of the US economy. Additionally, central bank meetings, particularly those of the Fed and the ECB, will be closely watched for any changes in monetary policy. The ongoing conflict in the Middle East and its impact on oil prices and global inflation will also remain a critical focus.

In conclusion, the current market environment is characterized by heightened risk aversion due to geopolitical tensions. Investors are advised to stay vigilant and monitor key economic data releases and central bank actions closely. The situation in the Middle East and its implications for global markets will continue to be a major point of concern. With central banks set to make rate decisions, the coming days will be crucial in determining the future market trajectory.

As we move forward, specific data to watch includes the PCE Core on March 28, the GDP QoQ on April 29, and the central bank rate decisions throughout the week. These will provide valuable insights into the state of the global economy and the potential responses of policymakers to the current challenges.

He also talks to Crisis Group’s Lebanon expert David Wood about the latest Israel-Hezbollah war.

articles.title: The Danger of Vibe Patriotism in Defense Tech
articles.snippet: At conferences, in pitch decks, and increasingly in public writing, “service” is increasingly being used to describe the work of startup founders, employees, and venture capital investors in defense technology. The claim, sometimes explicit, more often implied, is that building defense technology is itself a form of service comparable to uniformed military service.It is visible in corporate messaging that invites civilians to “serve” through product work, from Palantir’s “Why We Serve” to Anduril’s description of “national service as a form of innovation,” to public-private innovation narratives that describe entrepreneurship as a form of national service and “answering the call” advocacy The post The Danger of Vibe Patriotism in Defense Tech appeared first on War on the Rocks .

articles.title: Sinopec Slashes Refining Runs as Hormuz Disruption Squeezes Crude Supply
articles.snippet: Sinopec, China’s biggest oil refiner, has reduced its run rates by 10%, Bloomberg reported today, citing unnamed sources, in response to the supply squeeze resulting from the traffic disruption in the Strait of Hormuz. The size of the cut is equal to about half a million barrels daily. There will also be additional output losses from maintenance operations, the sources said. The refining major accounts for about a third of China’s total refined petroleum product output, with an average processing rate of 5.2 million barrels daily, the…

articles.title: Middle East Crisis Could Triple Pakistan’s Oil Import Bill
articles.snippet: Previously, we reported that Pakistan could find itself sucked into the Middle East war thanks to the Strategic Mutual Defence Agreement (SMDA) it signed with Saudi Arabia in September 2025. The core of the agreement states that any aggression against one country is considered an aggression against both, potentially requiring Pakistan to intervene against Iran’s missile strikes on Saudi Arabia. However, military confrontation with its southerly neighbor might turn out to be a less existential threat for Pakistan. Surging oil prices are already…

articles.title: How the Iran War Could Reshape Global Energy Policy
articles.snippet: The U.S.-Israeli attack on Iran and the ongoing conflict in the Middle East have caused the biggest oil disruption in history. The closure of a major transit route between Asia and Europe, as well as restrictions on fossil fuel production in the region, have driven oil and gas prices up, a trend that is set to continue. So, as governments are facing rising consumer energy bills once again, will it be enough to encourage a Covid-era pivot to renewable energy? The U.S. war against Iran has caused the largest oil disruption in history, according to…

articles.title: Oil Prices Remain Volatile as Hormuz Tensions Rise
articles.snippet: Oil markets remained volatile in early Asian trade on Monday as Trump attempts to form a broader coalition of countries to protect shipping through the Strait of Hormuz amid fresh attacks on energy infrastructure across the Middle East. At the time of writing, West Texas Intermediate had dipped 0.53% to $98.19 after an initial surge, while Brent was continuing its climb, rising 0.41% to $103.60 per barrel. The volatility follows a weekend of escalation in the Middle East conflict, beginning on Friday evening when the U.S. bombed military assets…

articles.title: EU Ministers Race to Shield Industry From an Escalating Energy Shock
articles.snippet: The European Union is in a rush to find ways to alleviate the energy cost pain for consumers and businesses, with energy ministers of member states set to meet later today to discuss options. These, according to Reuters, include boosting the supply of carbon permits to reduce their price, state support for industrial energy consumers, and tax cuts. The reportedly discussed options suggest European leaders are making a rare move to prioritize energy affordability over emission reduction. The option with greater availability of carbon permits is…
